title: OpenClaw v2026.5.27: Security Hardening & Provider Expansion
description: OpenClaw v2026.5.27 hardens security boundaries, cuts gateway overhead with metadata caching, expands providers with OpenAI embeddings and Pixverse video.
content:
# OpenClaw v2026.5.27: Security Hardening, Gateway Caching & Provider Expansion — What B2B Teams Need to Know

OpenClaw shipped v2026.5.27 on May 28, 2026 — a focused release that tightens security boundaries, reduces gateway overhead, expands the AI provider ecosystem, and hardens multi-channel delivery. If your team runs AI-driven outreach across WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, or Discord, here is what matters.

---

## What Changed in v2026.5.27

### 1. Security Hardening — Four Layers Added

This release ships four targeted security fixes that matter most to enterprise B2B deployments:

**Group prompt injection blocked.** Group prompt text is now kept strictly out of the system prompt, preventing malicious channel messages from injecting instructions into your AI agent's context.

**Hostname normalization.** Repeated-dot hostnames are normalized before routing — closing a class of URL confusion attacks that could redirect your agent's outbound calls to unintended endpoints.

**Command wrapper protection.** Side-effecting command wrappers that could silently mutate agent behavior are now blocked at the execution boundary.

**Node runtime safety.** Unsafe Node.js runtime environment overrides are blocked — relevant if you run OpenClaw in shared infrastructure or expose it to untrusted plugin authors.

> **Impact for B2B SDR teams:** If you run OpenClaw in multi-tenant environments or allow external contacts to interact with your sales agent, v2026.5.27's security hardening directly reduces your attack surface.

### 2. Gateway Performance — Less Hot-Path Rediscovery

Every millisecond of gateway latency costs conversion. v2026.5.27 reduces hot-path overhead by caching six categories of previously redundant lookups:

| What's cached now | Previous behavior |
|---|---|
| Session reads | Re-fetched on every request |
| Plugin metadata fingerprints | Rescanned on every load |
| Auth env snapshots | Rebuilt from disk each time |
| Auto-enabled plugin config | Computed on every trigger |
| Tool-search catalogs | Rebuilt per query |
| Stable metadata caches | No persistence between calls |

The cumulative effect: agents respond faster under load, plugin resolution overhead drops, and tools like lead enrichment or CRM lookup complete sooner.

### 3. Codex App-Server Reliability

Codex — the embedded AI coding and automation runtime — receives four reliability improvements:

- **Model resolution fixed**: The app-server now correctly identifies and routes to the configured model, preventing silent failures in automation workflows.
- **Workspace memory routing**: Memory operations are correctly scoped to the active workspace, preventing cross-workspace bleed in multi-team deployments.
- **Client persistence**: Codex clients now survive network interruptions better — less task loss when outreach sequences are running.
- **Relay generation preserved**: Generated relay configs are no longer dropped on restart, keeping automation pipelines intact across deployments.

### 4. Provider Expansion — OpenAI Embeddings, DeepInfra, Pixverse

Three new provider capabilities ship in v2026.5.27:

**OpenAI-compatible embeddings.** Any provider that speaks the OpenAI embeddings API can now be registered — this includes self-hosted models (via Ollama), Cohere, Jina, and others. Use cases for B2B SDR: semantic lead deduplication, intent classification across long conversation threads, and RAG-powered company research.

**DeepInfra catalog browsing.** The full DeepInfra model catalog is now browsable from the model picker — giving teams access to open-weight models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen) without manually specifying model IDs.

**Pixverse video generation.** Pixverse video generation is now a native provider option. Early use case for B2B: personalized video introductions or product demos delivered as outreach attachments on WhatsApp and LinkedIn.

> **Migration note:** Memory-specific embedding registration is now deprecated in favor of the general embedding provider API. Migrate before the next major release.

### 5. Channel Delivery Stability

Six channels receive reliability fixes: **Telegram**, **iMessage**, **Slack**, **Matrix**, **Discord**, and **Google Chat**. For outreach teams, channel reliability directly affects deliverability rates — dropped messages mean missed leads.

---

## Migration Notes

- **Deprecation**: Memory-specific embedding provider registration is deprecated. Move to the general `embeddingProviders` API.
- **Security**: If your plugins use custom Node.js runtime env overrides, audit for compatibility with the new safety guards.
- **No breaking changes** for standard SDR deployments.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Does v2026.5.27 require any config changes?**
For most deployments, no. The only required migration is if you use memory-specific embedding registration — switch to the general `embeddingProviders` API before the next major release.

**Will the security changes affect my existing plugins?**
Only if your plugins use unsafe Node.js runtime env overrides or side-effecting command wrappers. Standard PulseAgent-approved plugins require no changes.

**What is Pixverse video generation used for in B2B?**
Early adopters are using it for personalized video intro messages on WhatsApp and LinkedIn. The native provider integration means video generation happens inside your OpenClaw workflow without external API wiring.

**How does OpenAI-compatible embedding support help SDR teams?**
It enables semantic search over your lead database using any compatible embedding model — useful for deduplicating inbound contacts, finding similar leads, and powering intent-based routing.

**Which channels were fixed in this release?**
Telegram, iMessage, Slack, Matrix, Discord, and Google Chat all received delivery reliability fixes in v2026.5.27.
